title: Dart Game

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-04-probability-theory.md

setup.narrative: Jason throws darts at a target. Each throw lands at a random distance from the center, with all distance distributions identical and independent across throws.
rules: All throws are i.i.d. random distances.
Each subsequent throw (after the first) is FARTHER from the center than the FIRST throw.
setup.question: What is the probability the new dart is also farther from the center than the first throw?

learningObjectives: Reframe the problem in terms of rankings of n+1 i.i.d. throws.
Recognize that 'the (n+1)-th throw is not the best' is equivalent to the desired event.
Use uniform-rank symmetry to compute P = n/(n+1).

stages.label: Reframe in terms of rankings
stages.prompt: Let throws be ranked 1 (best, closest to center) through n+1 (worst). What's the equivalent question in terms of rankings?

options.text: Given that throws 2..n are all worse than throw 1 (so throw 1 is rank 1 of the first n), what is P(throw n+1 is also worse than throw 1)?
What is P(throw n+1 is rank 1)?
What is P(all throws have the same distance)?

stages.explanation: 'Throw 1 is best of the first n' means throw 1 has rank 1 of the first n. We want throw n+1 to be worse than throw 1 -- equivalently, throw 1 is still the rank-1 throw among ALL n+1.

stages.label: Use rank symmetry
stages.prompt: Conditional on the orderings of the first n throws (i.e., conditional on throw 1 being the best of the first n), where does the (n+1)-th throw rank among ALL n+1 throws?

options.text: By symmetry across i.i.d. samples, the (n+1)-th throw is equally likely to be in any of the n+1 rank positions among all n+1 throws.
It's always between throws 2 and n.

stages.explanation: Symmetry: i.i.d. samples are exchangeable. Each of the n+1 throws is equally likely to be the OVERALL best (rank 1). Conditional on the first n's ordering doesn't break this symmetry.

stages.label: Compute the probability
stages.prompt: P(throw n+1 is worse than throw 1) = P(throw n+1 is NOT rank 1 of all n+1). What's that?
stages.explanation: P(throw n+1 is rank 1) = 1/(n+1) by symmetry. Complement: P(not rank 1) = 1 - 1/(n+1) = n/(n+1).

stages.label: Commit for the original n=2 case
stages.prompt: For the original problem, n = 2 (two prior throws have already been thrown). P(third throw is also farther than the first) = n/(n+1) = ?
stages.explanation: P = 2/3 ≈ 0.6667. The intuition: throws 2 and 3 are 'tied' from the perspective of ranking, both being i.i.d. with throw 1. Given throw 2 was worse than throw 1, throw 3 has probability 2/3 of also being worse.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Verify the n=2 case by enumerating all 6 orderings of throws 1, 2, 3.
What's the probability the (n+1)-th throw is the BEST of all n+1?
Why does the answer not depend on the distribution of the throws (only that they're i.i.d.)?
